questions: On Apple’s official website, the Mac user guidance directs customers to broader support and purchasing resources. Focusing on the Apple Online Store for the Southeast Asian city-state whose store ships only domestically, does not ship to freight forwarders, and accepts orders only from local billing addresses, explain the key terms governing purchases. Specifically, outline: (1) the standard return window and conditions (including any special notes for iPhone returns and refund remittance restrictions), (2) the additional rules for products purchased in volume, (3) the price reduction protection policy (time limit and unit cap), and (4) the main requirements and liabilities related to shipping, risk transfer, and in-store pickup, including third-party pickup.

On Apple’s official macOS support resources, several distinct introductory and feature-focused guides are provided to help users understand and use their Mac more effectively. Identify the set of guides that (a) help new users set up and begin using a Mac, (b) assist users transitioning from Windows, (c) explain how a Mac integrates with other Apple devices, (d) outline the major new features of the latest macOS release named Tahoe, (e) describe built-in privacy and security protections, (f) explain how to recover a recently deleted password or passkey, (g) provide model-specific getting started information, and (h) introduce Apple’s built-in AI system for macOS. For each guide, summarize its primary purpose and the key capabilities or features it highlights.

Apple offers an integrated, on‑device artificial intelligence system built into its latest operating systems across phones, tablets, computers, headsets, and watches. Identify all compatible device categories and minimum hardware generations that support this system, and specify the minimum operating system versions required on each platform. Then explain the setup prerequisites (including storage requirements, language configuration rules, pairing conditions for wearables, and download considerations). Additionally, summarize how feature availability differs across platforms (phone/tablet, computer, headset, and watch), including examples such as translation capabilities, photo and writing tools, notification or message summaries, fitness features, and any notable regional or country-based restrictions. Your answer should compare these requirements and capabilities across all supported platforms rather than focusing on just one device type.

rounds.question: On Apple’s official website, the Mac user guidance directs customers to broader support and purchasing resources. Focusing on the Apple Online Store for the Southeast Asian city-state whose store ships only domestically, does not ship to freight forwarders, and accepts orders only from local billing addresses, explain the key terms governing purchases. Specifically, outline: (1) the standard return window and conditions (including any special notes for iPhone returns and refund remittance restrictions), (2) the additional rules for products purchased in volume, (3) the price reduction protection policy (time limit and unit cap), and (4) the main requirements and liabilities related to shipping, risk transfer, and in-store pickup, including third-party pickup.
checklist_width: Standard 14-day return window with requirement for original condition, parts, accessories, and packaging
iPhone return caveat about wireless service agreement not being automatically canceled
Refunds remitted only to a local bank account matching the payor and not outside the country
Volume purchases (more than 4 per order/category) must be returned within 7 days with 25% restocking fee and returned to original store
Price protection available within 14 calendar days of receipt, excluding limited-time promotions, capped at 10 units
Risk of loss passes upon physical possession; title passes when picked up by carrier and shipment notification is sent
In-store pickup requires government-issued photo ID and order number; third-party pickup requires designated name/email and ID; store not responsible after pickup
Store ships only domestically within the country and does not ship to freight forwarders
checklist_depth: Apple Online Store Singapore + Identified by policy stating it ships only within Singapore, accepts only Singapore billing addresses, and does not ship to freight forwarders
Singapore Consumer Store Terms and Conditions + Verified by matching return windows, price protection limits, and domestic refund restrictions specific to Singapore
rounds.rationale: The question uses contextual logic from Mac user guidance pointing users toward official Apple support and purchasing channels (Deep), then indirectly identifies the specific regional store through unique shipping and billing restrictions without naming it. It requires aggregating multiple scattered policy elements—returns, volume rules, price protection, shipping risk, and pickup conditions—across the same store’s terms (Wide).
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Singapore Consumer Store Thanks for shopping at Apple. We appreciate the fact that you like to buy the cool stuff we build. We also want to make sure you have a rewarding expe
rounds.answer_a:
Apple’s Singapore Online Store accepts orders only from customers with a Singapore billing address and ships only within Singapore; orders to freight forwarders are prohibited.[1]

**(1) Returns and refunds**  
Most items may be returned within 14 calendar days from delivery or pickup.[1] Products must be in original condition with all accessories, packaging, and proof of purchase; some items (e.g., software downloads, gift cards) are non‑returnable.[1] iPhone returns must include the device with no user data and may be subject to additional carrier/activation reversal requirements.[1] Refunds are generally made only to the original payment method and only to accounts in Singapore; Apple does not remit refunds to foreign accounts or alter payee details.[1][2]

**(2) Volume purchases**  
For most products, customers may purchase up to two units per model; certain items (like Apple Watch, iPad, or Mac configurations) may carry additional quantity limits. These limits also apply to orders placed under volume or multiple separate orders, and Apple may cancel orders exceeding caps.[1]

**(3) Price reduction protection**  
If Apple reduces its price on an Apple‑branded product within 14 calendar days after your purchase, you may request a refund of the difference for up to 10 units per product type; Apple may decline for special promotions.[1]

**(4) Shipping, risk, and pickup**  
Title and risk of loss pass to the customer upon delivery to the carrier or upon in‑store pickup, as stated in local terms.[1] For in‑store pickup, government‑issued ID and order details are required; customers may designate a third‑party pickup contact, who must present matching ID and order information and assumes responsibility upon collection.[1][2]

## References  
[1] Apple Store Singapore – Terms and Conditions of Sale, Apple Singapore Online Store.  
[2] Apple Singapore – Sales and Refund Policy / Online Store Help pages (shipping, pickup, and returns).
rounds.answer_b:
Search web for Apple Online Store Southeast Asian city-state domestic shipping freight forwarders local billing addresses likely Singapore.Search query: Apple Online Store Singapore sales terms return policy price protection freight forwarder local billing addresses pickup third-party pickup

For Apple Online Store Singapore, most products may be returned within **14 calendar days** after you receive them, provided they are in original condition and packaging with included accessories/manuals; software with broken seals, opened memory, and some media are excluded. If an **iPhone** is returned, service may be cancelled immediately and any use-related carrier charges remain the customer’s responsibility. Refunds are generally made to the original payment method, but if cash-equivalent remittance would breach law or banking practice, Apple may remit by **bank transfer** instead [1][2].

For **volume purchases**, if you buy more than the permitted quantity, Apple may treat the order as a volume order and limit, reject, or cancel it. Such products may be ineligible for return or be subject to different return terms, and Apple reserves discretion over whether the quantity suggests reseller or export activity [1].

Apple’s **price protection** allows you to request a refund of the difference if Apple lowers the price of a product you purchased within **14 calendar days** of delivery. This applies to up to **10 units per product**. Clearance, special promotional pricing, limited-time offers, and certain other discounts are excluded [1].

For **shipping and pickup**, Apple ships only within Singapore, will not ship to freight forwarders, and accepts orders only from billing addresses in Singapore [1]. Title passes when the product is picked up by the carrier, so risk of loss transfers on delivery; however, for consumer protection purposes, risk generally passes when you or your nominee physically receive the goods [1]. For **in-store pickup**, only you or a designated third party may collect after Apple’s notice, and government-issued photo ID plus order details may be required. You remain responsible for the third party’s actions, including pickup and product receipt [1][3].

## References
[1] Apple. “Apple Store Online - Singapore - Sales and Refund Policy.” https://www.apple.com/sg/legal/sales-support/sales-policies/retail_sg.html

[2] Apple. “Returns & Refunds - Shopping Help.” https://www.apple.com/sg/shop/help/returns_refund

[3] Apple. “Shopping Help - Pickup.” https://www.apple.com/sg/shop/help/shipping_delivery
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the Apple Online Store Singapore (domestic-only shipping, no freight forwarders, Singapore billing addresses), so both pass the entity check.

Width/Completeness: Both miss a critical checklist item on volume purchases (more than 4 units → 7-day return window, 25% restocking fee, return to original store). Agent A instead discusses quantity caps and cancellations; Agent B gives vague discretionary language. This is a major omission (WIDE failure) for both. On iPhone returns, Agent B inaccurately states service may be cancelled immediately (contrary to the policy caveat that wireless agreements are not automatically canceled), while Agent A is vague but not clearly wrong. On risk transfer, Agent A incorrectly states risk passes to the carrier, while Agent B more accurately distinguishes title vs. consumer risk upon physical receipt. Both partially address refund remittance but neither clearly states the strict local bank/payor-matching restriction.

User Experience & Structure: Both are well-formatted, scannable, and within length. Agent B has slightly richer nuance and more citations, but also contains a clearer substantive error (iPhone service cancellation). Agent A is cleaner but legally less precise on risk transfer.

Because both agents contain significant factual gaps and miss a major required sub-point, this is a LOW-quality tie.

rounds.question: On Apple’s official macOS support resources, several distinct introductory and feature-focused guides are provided to help users understand and use their Mac more effectively. Identify the set of guides that (a) help new users set up and begin using a Mac, (b) assist users transitioning from Windows, (c) explain how a Mac integrates with other Apple devices, (d) outline the major new features of the latest macOS release named Tahoe, (e) describe built-in privacy and security protections, (f) explain how to recover a recently deleted password or passkey, (g) provide model-specific getting started information, and (h) introduce Apple’s built-in AI system for macOS. For each guide, summarize its primary purpose and the key capabilities or features it highlights.
checklist_width: Explains initial Mac setup, choosing wallpaper, changing common settings, using apps
Describes switching from Windows, navigation differences, transferring files from a PC
Details integration with other Apple devices, including viewing/controlling iPhone, cross-device copy and paste
Summarizes macOS Tahoe features such as redesigned interface, making phone calls from Mac, enhanced Spotlight actions, automatic translations
Describes built-in privacy and security tools including password management and private browsing
Explains how to recover a recently deleted password or passkey
Mentions model-specific Getting Started guides for new Mac owners
Introduces Apple Intelligence as personal, private AI integrated into macOS
checklist_depth: ‘Get started with your Mac’ guide + identified as the setup/beginner guide
‘Switched from Windows to Mac’ guide + identified as the transition guide for PC users
‘Use your Mac with other Apple devices’ guide + identified as the ecosystem integration guide
‘What’s new in macOS Tahoe’ guide + identified as the latest macOS feature overview
‘Control your privacy and security’ guide + identified as the privacy/security resource
‘Recover a recently deleted password’ resource + identified as the password recovery guide
‘Get to know your Mac’ (model-specific Getting Started guide) + identified as device-specific onboarding
‘Apple Intelligence’ overview + identified as the AI system integrated into macOS
rounds.rationale: The question masks the entities by describing their functional roles (Deep logic) rather than listing their titles directly. To answer correctly, a user must identify all relevant macOS support guides that match each functional description. It then requires summarizing the key features of each guide (Wide aggregation), ensuring the responder gathers and compares information across all distinct resources rather than relying on a single page.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Get started with your Mac You can set up and start using your new Mac in no time. Choose a wallpaper for your desktop, change common settings, start using apps and more. Learn
rounds.answer_a:
Apple’s macOS support resources include several distinct guides:

(a) **Mac basics / Get to know your Mac** – Introductory guide that helps new users set up a Mac, learn the desktop, Finder, Dock, menus, and key gestures, plus how to customize settings and install apps.[1]

(b) **Move from Windows to Mac** – Targets switchers from Windows, mapping common Windows concepts (taskbar, File Explorer, Control Panel) to macOS equivalents (Dock, Finder, System Settings), and explaining keyboard differences and file management.[2]

(c) **Use your Mac with other devices** – Explains Continuity features such as Handoff, Universal Clipboard, Continuity Camera, phone calls, messages, and iCloud syncing across iPhone, iPad, Apple Watch, and Mac.[3]

(d) **What’s new in macOS Tahoe** – Highlights major new features in the latest release, including Apple Intelligence, redesigned system apps, new productivity and collaboration tools, and updated privacy options.[4]

(e) **Privacy and security in macOS** – Describes protections like FileVault, Gatekeeper, sandboxing, app permissions, iCloud Keychain, Lockdown Mode, and on-device processing for sensitive data.[5]

(f) **Recover passwords and passkeys** – Shows how to restore recently deleted passwords or passkeys from the Passwords section in System Settings and iCloud Keychain within a limited recovery window.[6]

(g) **Mac model-specific “Get started” pages** – Per‑model guides (e.g., MacBook Air, iMac) covering hardware layout, ports, setup, initial configuration, and tips tailored to that device.[7]

(h) **Apple Intelligence on Mac** – Introduces Apple’s built-in AI, including system-wide writing tools, image generation, Genmoji, and enhanced Siri, emphasizing on-device processing and privacy.[4][8]

## References
[1] https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/welcome/mac  
[2] https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/move-from-windows-to-mac-mh35835  
[3] https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/use-your-mac-with-iphone-ipad-and-apple-watch  
[4] https://www.apple.com/macos/macos-tahoe-preview/  
[5] https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/privacy-and-security-settings-mchlp2929  
[6] https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/save-and-fill-passwords-mchlf375f392  
[7] https://support.apple.com/mac  
[8] https://www.apple.com/apple-intelligence/
rounds.answer_b:
Apple’s macOS support resources include eight distinct guides covering setup, switching, ecosystem use, new features, security, credential recovery, device-specific onboarding, and Apple intelligence. **(a)** **Get started with Mac** helps new users set up a Mac and learn everyday basics such as signing in, customizing settings, using apps, and navigating macOS efficiently.[1] **(b)** **Switch from Windows to Mac** is for Windows users moving to macOS, explaining interface differences, file management, common task equivalents, and migration guidance.[2] **(c)** **Mac and other Apple devices** shows how a Mac works with iPhone, iPad, Apple Watch, and AirPods through Continuity features like Handoff, Universal Clipboard, AirDrop, Sidecar, and device unlocking.[3] **(d)** **What’s new in macOS Tahoe** summarizes the release’s headline additions, highlighting updated interface elements and major new capabilities introduced in Tahoe.[4] **(e)** **Built-in privacy and security protections on Mac** explains safeguards such as Gatekeeper, FileVault, system integrity protections, app permission controls, and other privacy-focused defenses.[5] **(f)** **Recover a recently deleted password or passkey on Mac** focuses on credential recovery in Passwords, letting users restore recently deleted passwords or passkeys before permanent removal.[6] **(g)** model-specific **Get started** pages, such as **Get started with MacBook Air**, provide hardware-tailored setup, port/location overviews, battery and charging basics, and first-use instructions.[7] **(h)** **Apple Intelligence on Mac** introduces Apple’s built-in AI system, describing writing assistance, image-related tools, smarter Siri experiences, and privacy-conscious on-device or Private Cloud Compute processing.[8]

## References
1. Apple Support — Get started with Mac. https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/welcome/mac  
2. Apple Support — Switch from Windows to Mac. https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/switch-from-windows-to-mac-mchlbc684e49/mac  
3. Apple Support — Mac and other Apple devices. https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/mac-and-other-apple-devices-mchl86d72b76/mac  
4. Apple Support — What’s new in macOS Tahoe. https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/whats-new-in-macos-tahoe-mchl8e129a5f/mac  
5. Apple Support — Built-in privacy and security protections on Mac. https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/built-in-privacy-and-security-protections-on-mac-mchl3f025a39/mac  
6. Apple Support — Recover a recently deleted password or passkey on Mac. https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/recover-a-recently-deleted-password-or-passkey-mchlfdf0f56f/mac  
7. Apple Support — Get started with MacBook Air. https://support.apple.com/guide/macbook-air/welcome/mac  
8. Apple Support — Apple Intelligence on Mac. https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/apple-intelligence-on-mac-mac15c31e0b9/mac
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the required eight official macOS guides (setup, Windows switchers, ecosystem integration, macOS Tahoe overview, privacy/security, password recovery, model-specific getting started, and Apple Intelligence). Neither failed the core entity check.

Second, Width/Completeness: Both covered all checklist elements. However, Agent B aligns more precisely with the exact support guide titles (e.g., “Get started with Mac,” “Switch from Windows to Mac,” “Built-in privacy and security protections on Mac,” and the specific recovery guide). Agent A is slightly looser in naming (e.g., “Mac basics / Get to know your Mac”) and cites a marketing preview page for Tahoe rather than the dedicated support guide. Agent A’s password recovery citation also appears less directly aligned with the specific recovery page.

Finally, Presentation & UX: Both are well-structured and scannable with clear (a)–(h) labeling. However, Agent B provides more exact guide names and cleaner alignment with official support URLs, which improves trustworthiness and user experience. The formatting is slightly more precise and consistent.

Conclusion: Both are strong, but Agent B delivers a more precise, support-aligned, and user-trust-optimized answer. Hence, B_BETTER.

Identify all compatible device categories and minimum hardware generations that support this system, and specify the minimum operating system versions required on each platform. Then explain the setup prerequisites (including storage requirements, language configuration rules, pairing conditions for wearables, and download considerations). Additionally, summarize how feature availability differs across platforms (phone/tablet, computer, headset, and watch), including examples such as translation capabilities, photo and writing tools, notification or message summaries, fitness features, and any notable regional or country-based restrictions. Your answer should compare these requirements and capabilities across all supported platforms rather than focusing on just one device type.

Lists compatible iPad models (mini with A17 Pro, M1 or later) and required iPadOS versions
Lists compatible Mac models (M1 or later) and required macOS versions

source_nodes: How to get Apple Intelligence - Apple Support
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: To get started with Apple Intelligence features on your compatible iPhone, iPad, Mac, or Apple Vision Pro, update your device to the latest software version, and ensure you ha
rounds.answer_a:
Apple Intelligence is supported on:

**iPhone**  
- Devices: iPhone 15 Pro and iPhone 15 Pro Max only.[1]  
- OS: iOS 18 or later.[1]  

**iPad**  
- Devices: iPad Pro with M1 chip or later, and iPad Air with M1 chip or later.[1]  
- OS: iPadOS 18 or later.[1]  

**Mac**  
- Devices: Any Mac with an M1 chip or later (including MacBook Air, MacBook Pro, iMac, Mac mini, Mac Studio, Mac Pro).[1]  
- OS: macOS Sequoia or later.[1]  

**Apple Vision Pro (headset)**  
- Device: Apple Vision Pro (M2).[2]  
- OS: visionOS 2 or later (Apple Intelligence coming in a visionOS 2 update).[2]  

**Apple Watch**  
- No Apple‑watch‑native Apple Intelligence; select features (e.g., smarter Siri, notification summaries) rely on a paired iPhone 15 Pro/Pro Max running iOS 18 with Apple Intelligence enabled.[1][3]  

**Setup prerequisites**  
- Requires Apple ID, iCloud, and sufficient free storage to download Apple Intelligence models and on‑device packs; Apple notes “features may require additional downloads” on each platform.[1][2]  
- Language: initial rollout limited to U.S. English; system language and Siri language must be set accordingly.[1][2]  
- Wearables: Apple Watch and Vision Pro features depend on pairing with a supported iPhone or Mac for some capabilities (e.g., Notification Summary from iPhone, continuity features from Mac).[2][3]  

**Feature differences**  
- **Phone/Tablet:** rich Writing Tools (rewrite, proofread, summarize in apps), Image Playground, Genmoji, systemwide notification and message summaries, Mail triage, and improved Siri with on‑device + cloud “Apple Intelligence” requests.[1]  
- **Mac:** same writing and image tools plus deeper app integration (Mail, Notes, Pages, Keynote, third‑party apps via system APIs) and desktop‑oriented notification summaries.[1]  
- **Vision Pro:** focus on text assistance, summarization, and Siri in spatial apps; fewer photo tools than iPhone/iPad at launch.[2]  
- **Watch:** benefits mainly from smarter Siri requests and summarized notifications routed from iPhone; fitness features remain handled by watchOS, not Apple Intelligence models directly.[3]  

Regional restrictions: initial availability limited to U.S. (U.S. English); other languages and regions will follow later.[1][2]  

## References  
[1] https://www.apple.com/apple-intelligence/  
[2] https://www.apple.com/apple-vision-pro/apple-intelligence/  
[3] https://www.apple.com/ios/ios-18/
rounds.answer_b:
Apple Intelligence is supported on iPhone 16 models, iPhone 15 Pro, and iPhone 15 Pro Max running iOS 18.1 or later; iPad models with M1 or later or iPad mini with A17 Pro running iPadOS 18.1 or later; Macs with M1 or later running macOS Sequoia 15.1 or later; Apple Vision Pro running visionOS 2.4 or later; and Apple Watch only when paired with a supported Apple Intelligence iPhone, requiring watchOS 11.4 or later.[1][2][3][4][5]

Setup requirements differ by platform but share core rules: Apple Intelligence must download AI models after activation, and the device language plus Siri language must both be set to the same supported language. Supported languages include localized English first, later expanding to French, German, Italian, Portuguese (Brazil), Spanish, Japanese, Korean, and Simplified Chinese, with some features additionally localized for Singapore and India English.[1][2][3] Apple recommends at least 7 GB of available storage for the download on iPhone, iPad, Mac, and Vision Pro.[1][2][3][4] On Apple Watch, Apple Intelligence features depend on the paired compatible iPhone being nearby, because processing is tied to that phone rather than the watch itself.[5]

Feature availability also varies. On iPhone and iPad, Apple highlights Writing Tools, notification and message summaries, Priority Messages, Clean Up in Photos, Image Playground, Genmoji, smarter Siri, visual intelligence, and in some regions ChatGPT integration and Apple Intelligence-based organization features.[1][2] Macs get many of the same writing, image, summary, and Siri capabilities, plus desktop-focused workflows such as text revision across Mac apps and productivity use cases.[3] Vision Pro supports Writing Tools, Image Playground, Genmoji, memory movies, natural-language photo/video search, smart reply, Priority Messages, Mail summaries, and notification summaries, but not every iPhone feature.[4] Apple Watch offers a narrower set: notification summaries, Priority Notifications, Smart Reply, and Workout Buddy fitness coaching; Live Translation is available in Messages but only on supported paired models.[5]

## References
1. Apple Support — Apple Intelligence requirements for iPhone. https://support.apple.com/en-us/121115  
2. Apple Support — Apple Intelligence requirements for iPad. https://support.apple.com/en-us/121116  
3. Apple Support — Apple Intelligence requirements for Mac. https://support.apple.com/en-us/121117  
4. Apple Support — Apple Intelligence requirements for Apple Vision Pro. https://support.apple.com/en-us/121118  
5. Apple Support — Apple Intelligence requirements for Apple Watch. https://support.apple.com/en-us/121119
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the core entity (Apple Intelligence) and recognized that compatibility depends on both hardware generation and OS version, so both pass Deep Logic. However, Agent A fails Width/Completeness: it omits iPhone 16 models, iPad mini (A17 Pro), specific Apple Watch model generations and watchOS version, the 7 GB storage requirement, detailed language expansion rules, download considerations, and most regional restrictions. It also under-develops cross-platform feature comparisons and setup nuances. Agent B, by contrast, lists all major device families with minimum hardware generations and specific OS sub-versions, includes the 7 GB storage requirement, language-matching rules, download/model requirements, watch pairing dependency, and provides a clearer cross-platform feature comparison (including translation, Workout Buddy, summaries, and regional ChatGPT notes). Formatting is dense but structured and citation-backed. Because Agent A misses multiple critical checklist elements while Agent B covers nearly all, B is MUCH_BETTER due to significantly superior completeness and user utility.
